subsystem: w3m HTML rendering engine — HTMLtagproc1(), the per-tag dispatcher that processes a single already-parsed HTML tag while laying out text for the terminal browser. It mutates the render buffer (obuf), the block-environment stack (envs), and per-tag state, emitting formatting commands (bold/italic, list markers, blank lines, anchors, tables, forms) as it walks a document.
provenance: Identified by the function signature HTMLtagproc1(struct parsed_tag *tag, struct html_feed_environ *h_env) and the pervasive w3m idioms: struct readbuffer *obuf, struct environment *envs, the HTML_* tag-id enum (HTML_B, HTML_UL, HTML_TABLE, ...), the ATTR_* attribute ids, RB_* render-buffer flag bits, and helper/macro names (HTMLlineproc1, parsedtag_get_value, PUSH_ENV/POP_ENV, CLOSE_A, Strnew_charp, html_quote). These are the exact names from w3m's file.c. The banner comment '/* sample: primevul_c_train_3370_vuln */' marks it as a PrimeVul C training sample.
trust_boundary: The tag argument is fully attacker-controlled: it is the tokenized form of an HTML tag taken from a fetched web page or local HTML file, so tag->tagid selects the case and every parsedtag_get_value(tag, ATTR_*, ...) returns attacker-chosen attribute values (URLs, list start/type, table width/border/cellspacing, titles, charset, meta refresh, etc.). h_env carries the mutable layout state (obuf, envs, envc/envc_real/nenv, table_level) that this and sibling calls accumulate across the whole document, so the ATTACK is the SEQUENCE and NESTING of tags an attacker feeds, not just one tag's fields. The envs[] block-environment array and the tables[]/table_mode[] arrays are the hostile-reachable memory; their index counters (envc, table_level) are driven directly by attacker tag nesting.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The function takes a parsed tag and the HTML feed environment. It caches two pointers out of h_env: obuf (the render/output buffer that accumulates the laid-out line and formatting flags) and envs (an array of 'environment' records, one per open block-level container such as a list or blockquote). p/q/r are scratch char* used to receive attribute-value strings from parsedtag_get_value; count/width/i/w/x/y/z are scratch integers for numeric attributes. cmd = tag->tagid selects which HTML element this call handles; it is the attacker-chosen tag id and drives the giant switch below. Nothing here is validated yet — this is pure setup.

If the buffer is currently in preformatted mode (RB_PRE flag set), a handful of inline-control tags (NOBR/PRE_INT and their closers) are swallowed and processed no further (return 1). This is a mode gate: inside <pre> those tags must not alter word-wrap state. It reads obuf->flag, which is accumulated document state.

The main dispatch begins. Bold and italic are handled with nesting counters (in_bold, in_italic): an opening tag increments the counter and only the outermost open emits a formatting effect (return 0 means 'let the caller also push the visible marker'; return 1 means 'handled, suppress'). The close tags decrement and, when the count reaches zero and close_effect0 confirms the matching open effect is on the stack, clear the effect. This counter design tolerates malformed/over-nested markup without underflowing (the >0 guards).

Underline mirrors bold/italic with in_under. The 'semantic' inline tags EM/STRONG/Q are rewritten into the physical tags <i>/<b>/quote characters by re-feeding a CONSTANT string back through HTMLlineproc1, which re-lexes and re-dispatches it (so <em> effectively becomes <i>). Because these strings are compile-time constants, the re-entrancy here is bounded; the same mechanism becomes a risk later when the re-fed string is attacker-derived.

Paragraph open/close: CLOSE_A macro closes any open anchor; unless paragraph breaks are being ignored, it flushes the current line and emits a blank line at the current environment's indent. Note envs[h_env->envc].indent — the indent is read from the environment record at index envc, the top of the block-environment stack. RB_IGNORE_P is set to collapse consecutive paragraph breaks. For a real

it also parses alignment and sets RB_P. This is the first of ~40 uses of envs[h_env->envc] as the current indent source.

Opening a list or blockquote pushes a new block environment via PUSH_ENV(cmd), which (in the implementation not shown) increments h_env->envc and initializes envs[envc]. Crucially this PUSH_ENV has NO 'envc_real < nenv' guard around it. Immediately after, envs[h_env->envc].count and .type are written using attacker attributes: ATTR_START sets the starting counter (count-1), ATTR_TYPE sets the bullet/number style (envs[...].type = (int)*p, the first character of the attacker string). Every access here is at index envc, the freshly pushed slot.

The list/blockquote closers pop an environment. They correctly guard with 'if (h_env->envc > 0)' before reading envs[h_env->envc - 1] and calling POP_ENV, so the pop side cannot underflow envc below zero. This is the symmetric counterpart to PUSH_ENV: an attacker who opens more containers than they close leaves envc elevated; one who closes more than opened is stopped by the envc>0 guard. HTML_DL open (below) is another unguarded push.

Definition-list open. Like UL/OL/BLQ it calls PUSH_ENV(cmd) with no nenv guard, then writes envs[h_env->envc].env. A <dl compact> switches the env tag to HTML_DL_COMPACT which changes later indent behavior. Another attacker-nestable push into the envs stack.

List item. Only processed when envc>0 (there is an enclosing list). It bumps the current list counter envs[envc].count, or overrides it from the attacker's ATTR_VALUE via atoi(p) (clamped: negative/zero becomes 0). It then branches on the enclosing environment's env kind (UL vs OL) read from envs[envc].env. For UL it emits INDENT_INCR-3 non-breaking spaces then a bullet symbol. All indices are envc/envc-1; envc-1 is safe under the envc>0 guard.

The UL bullet glyph is chosen from the attacker-controlled type ('d'/'c'/'s'), and for the default case from a per-depth table UL_SYMBOL((envc_real-1) % MAX_UL_LEVEL). The modulo bounds the symbol index regardless of how deep envc_real has climbed, so this table lookup is safe even at extreme nesting. Note envc_real (the 'true' depth counter) is used here whereas envc (the capped/allocated depth) is used for the envs[] slot — the divergence between these two counters is central to the risk section.

Ordered-list item numbering: the marker string num is built from the counter via romanNumeral/romanAlphabet or decimal Sprintf, honoring the attacker's ATTR_TYPE. push_spaces(obuf, 1, INDENT_INCR - num->length) pads the marker to the indent width; if num is longer than INDENT_INCR the count passed is negative, which push_spaces must treat as 'no spaces' rather than a huge unsigned — an implementation contract worth confirming, though INDENT_INCR-num->length is a plain int here.

Definition term. This case is the key contrast: its PUSH_ENV(HTML_DL) IS gated on 'h_env->envc_real < h_env->nenv' — i.e. it refuses to push when the true depth has reached the allocated capacity nenv. HTML_DD (next) has the identical guard. That these two sites explicitly bound the push against nenv, while UL/OL/BLQ/DL/FRAMESET do not, is direct evidence that PUSH_ENV itself does not internally bound envc — the guard is the caller's responsibility, and most callers omit it.

Definition data. Same guarded PUSH_ENV as DT. It then reads envs[envc].env / .indent to decide compact vs normal layout, and for compact mode pads with push_spaces(obuf, 1, indent - obuf->pos). This case confirms the intended invariant: writes into envs[] must be preceded by ensuring envc_real stays below nenv.

TITLE handling. Opening sets RB_TITLE and arms end_tag so the tokenizer routes following text into the title. Closing calls process_n_title(tag) to obtain the accumulated title string and re-feeds it through HTMLlineproc1(tmp->ptr, ...). Here the re-fed string is DERIVED FROM DOCUMENT CONTENT, not a constant, so this is an attacker-influenced re-parse — flagged in the risk section. FRAMESET/FRAME (below) push environments and emit anchors from ATTR_SRC/ATTR_NAME.

FRAMESET is a THIRD unguarded PUSH_ENV(cmd) site (like UL/OL/BLQ/DL). Its closer N_FRAMESET pops under the envc>0 guard. FRAME turns the frame's src/name into a synthetic anchor; the attacker URL is passed through html_quote before being embedded in a Sprintf'd <a> tag, so quoting is the injection defense. Note push_charp uses get_strwidth(q) as the length, deriving the copy length from the (quoted) attacker string.

HR renders a horizontal rule by building a string in process_hr (using the current indent) and re-feeding it via HTMLlineproc1. PRE enters preformatted mode: it sets RB_PRE and RB_IGNORE_P and either flushes+blanks or fills the line to the current indent. These continue the pattern of reading envs[envc].indent for layout; PRE_INT (below) manipulates break points.

Anchor open. If an anchor is already open it is closed first. Then each anchor field (url/target/referer/title) is copied from the attacker attribute into a freshly allocated Str; accesskey takes the first character of the attribute (unsigned char)*p. The hseq is read from ATTR_HSEQ — an attacker-supplied sequence number that the code trusts to index/label links (below).

IMG renders alt/placeholder text via process_img and re-feeds it. IMG_ALT/INPUT_ALT stash attacker src/type/value/name strings and numeric top/bottom margins into obuf state; the margins are only widened (i > current). These are straightforward string copies into heap Str objects with no fixed buffer, so no overflow at this layer.

TABLE increments obuf->table_level and — importantly — checks 'if (obuf->table_level >= MAX_TABLE) break;' BEFORE using it to index tables[] and table_mode[]. This is the correctly-guarded counterpart to the envs stack: the bound check precedes the write. Border/width/cellspacing/cellpadding/vspace are read from attacker attributes and CLAMPED (negatives to 0, values above the MAX_* macros to the cap), so numeric attributes cannot drive out-of-range layout values. This case is a model of the checks the envs pushes lack.

ISINDEX synthesizes a search form from the attacker's prompt/action, html_quote-ing both before splicing them into an HTML string that is re-fed through HTMLlineproc1. Again quoting is the injection barrier and the re-fed string is attacker-influenced. META (below) parses charset and http-equiv refresh, and can request a page reconstruct.

META refresh: getMetaRefreshParam parses the attacker CONTENT into an interval and a target URL; the URL is html_quote'd and rendered as a link, and tag->need_reconstruct may be set to force a document rebuild. The refresh interval and URL are attacker-controlled but flow into Sprintf/HTMLlineproc1 as quoted strings/ints, not into fixed buffers.

DEL/INS/S (strike) toggle display effects using the in_strike/in_ins nesting counters with the same >0 underflow guards as bold/italic. Notably in HTML_N_DEL the DISPLAY_INS_DEL_NORMAL case has NO break before DISPLAY_INS_DEL_FONTIFY, so in NORMAL mode it emits the '[:DEL]' text AND then falls into the FONTIFY strike-close logic — a latent logic bug (missing break) that runs unintended code, though it is not memory-unsafe.

SUP/SUB emit '^'/'[' ']' markers. BGSOUND/EMBED/APPLET/BODY optionally surface hidden object URLs (src/archive/background) as quoted links when view_unseenobject is set. The tail deliberately FALLS THROUGH: HTML_BODY -> HTML_N_HEAD -> HTML_HEAD -> HTML_N_BODY, and N_HEAD closes an open title. The default returns 0 (tag not consumed). The trailing 'not reached' return is dead code after the switch.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: PRIMARY RISK — out-of-bounds access of the envs[] block-environment array via unbounded nesting. envs points to h_env->envs, an array allocated with h_env->nenv elements, indexed by h_env->envc. The HTML_UL/HTML_OL/HTML_BLQ case calls PUSH_ENV(cmd) with NO 'envc_real < nenv' guard, and immediately writes envs[h_env->envc].count / .type / .env (lines 139, 143, 145, 149) and reads envs[h_env->envc].indent (line 150). That the HTML_DT and HTML_DD cases DO guard their PUSH_ENV with 'h_env->envc_real < h_env->nenv' (lines 273 and 292) proves PUSH_ENV does not clamp envc internally — it is the caller's job, and this caller omits it. An attacker serves deeply nested lists/blockquotes (<ul><ul><ul>... or <blockquote> chains) so envc advances past nenv; the subsequent envs[envc] writes land beyond the allocation. Impact: attacker-influenced out-of-bounds heap write (env/count/type/indent fields), i.e. memory corruption from a hostile web page. Invariant: every PUSH_ENV must first ensure envc/envc_real stays strictly below nenv (or PUSH_ENV must clamp), exactly as DT/DD do. This is CWE-119/787.

Same defect class at the HTML_DL open site (and identically at HTML_FRAMESET line 328). PUSH_ENV(cmd) is issued with no nenv bound, then envs[h_env->envc].env is written (line 182). Definition lists and framesets are equally attacker-nestable, so they are additional entry points to the same envs[] overflow. Merged with the UL/OL/BLQ finding as one contract, but each site independently violates it and each must be fixed. Invariant: bound the push against nenv before writing envs[envc].

Re-parse / re-entrancy hazard. HTMLtagproc1 re-feeds attacker-DERIVED strings back into the HTML pipeline via HTMLlineproc1: the reconstructed title from process_n_title (line 321), the ISINDEX form built from ATTR_PROMPT/ATTR_ACTION (line 770), the META refresh link (line 808), and object URLs from BGSOUND/EMBED/APPLET/BODY (lines 965/975/985/995). HTMLlineproc1 re-lexes that text and can dispatch back into HTMLtagproc1, and each such call can itself push environments or open anchors. Although html_quote/html_unquote are applied to block tag-injection, the re-entrant path shares the same obuf/envs/table_level state and can be nested, so it both compounds the envs-stack growth above and risks unbounded recursion (CWE-674) if a re-fed fragment re-expands into more feedable content. Invariant: the reconstructed text must be inert (no re-openable containers) or the recursion depth must be capped.

envs[h_env->envc - 1] is read here and at lines 191 and 280. If envc were 0 this would index envs[-1], an out-of-bounds read. In the shown code all three sites are correctly gated by 'if (h_env->envc > 0)' (lines 158, 188, 278), so they are safe AS WRITTEN. The residual risk is coupling: these reads are only safe while envc is an accurate, in-range depth. If the envs overflow above corrupts the counter or the allocation, or if a future edit removes an envc>0 guard, envc-1 becomes an underflowing index. Invariant: envc must remain in [0, nenv) at all times, which is exactly what the unguarded pushes violate.

Missing break in HTML_N_DEL: the DISPLAY_INS_DEL_NORMAL arm has no break, so when displayInsDel == DISPLAY_INS_DEL_NORMAL the code emits ':DEL]' and then FALLS THROUGH into the FONTIFY branch, executing the in_strike close logic that was never opened in NORMAL mode. This is a latent logic defect (CWE-484 omitted break): it can decrement/close a strike effect that is not active and emit a spurious </s>. It is not directly memory-unsafe, but it is a correctness bug that mismatches the well-formed switches elsewhere in the same function (compare HTML_N_S at lines 884-903 which does break). Invariant: each display-mode arm should terminate with break unless fall-through is deliberate.

Attribute value first-character reads: ATTR_TYPE at lines 145 and 231, and ATTR_ACCESSKEY at line 505 ('obuf->anchor.accesskey = (unsigned char)*p;'), dereference *p where p is the attacker attribute string returned by parsedtag_get_value. This is only safe if parsedtag_get_value guarantees p is non-NULL and NUL-terminated whenever it returns true; for an empty attribute (type="") *p reads the terminating '\0', which is benign, and no length is derived from it. Low severity, listed for completeness — the contract to confirm is that parsedtag_get_value never yields a true result with a NULL/unterminated p.

summary: This is w3m's HTMLtagproc1 per-tag layout dispatcher processing fully attacker-controlled HTML. The dominant risk is an out-of-bounds access of the fixed-capacity envs[] block-environment stack: several environment-opening tags (UL/OL/BLQ at line 136, DL at 180, FRAMESET at 328) call PUSH_ENV with NO 'envc_real < nenv' bound, whereas HTML_DT/HTML_DD (lines 273, 292) do guard it — proving the bound is the caller's responsibility and is missing, so deeply nested markup can drive envc past nenv and corrupt heap memory (CWE-119/787). Secondary issues are re-entrant HTMLlineproc1 feeding of attacker-derived strings (recursion/state-compounding) and a missing-break logic bug in HTML_N_DEL. Table handling and numeric attributes are, by contrast, correctly bounds-checked and clamped.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Entry point; tag is fully attacker-controlled parsed HTML, h_env carries mutable cross-tag state.
Enum selector for the switch; out-of-range ids land in default and return 0 — no memory contract.
envs is the fixed-capacity (nenv) array indexed by envc that the unguarded pushes overflow.
Unbounded push then envs[envc] writes — primary OOB anchor.
Second unguarded push into envs[] via <dl> nesting.
Third unguarded push into envs[] via <frameset> nesting.
These pushes ARE guarded by 'envc_real < nenv'; they are the correct pattern and evidence the others are wrong.
All gated by 'if (h_env->envc > 0)', so no negative index as written; safety depends on envc staying in range.
Attacker-valued writes at the possibly-out-of-range envc slot — the OOB write payload.
Modulo MAX_UL_LEVEL bounds the symbol-table index regardless of depth.
Re-parse/re-entrancy of attacker-influenced content; recursion/state-compounding hazard.
Compile-time constant arguments; bounded, no attacker influence.
Bound is checked BEFORE tables[]/table_mode[] are indexed — correctly guarded.
Index guarded by the MAX_TABLE check above; within [0, MAX_TABLE).
Defensive clamps of attacker numeric attributes to safe ranges — a positive control.
Compute a display width from attacker ATTR_WIDTH; result feeds table width fields, not an index.
Copies attacker strings/ints into heap Str or scalars; no fixed-buffer copy at this layer.
First-char reads of attacker string; benign for empty value but depend on parsedtag_get_value's non-NULL/NUL contract — low.
Result clamped: >0 used as count, else 0; no unsafe use.
Applied as the tag-injection barrier before re-embedding into HTML strings.
Signed int difference; if negative, push_spaces must treat as zero — implementation contract, not shown to be violated here.
Fall-through executes strike-close logic in NORMAL mode — latent logic bug (CWE-484).
Bit manipulation of a state word; no bounds contract.
External state-close macros; no visible buffer contract in this file.
Intentional case fall-through affecting return value/title close; no memory unsafety.
Dead code ('not reached'); harmless.

findings.title: Out-of-bounds access of envs[] block-environment stack via unbounded PUSH_ENV nesting
findings.anchor: PUSH_ENV(cmd) at HTML_UL/HTML_OL/HTML_BLQ, and envs[h_env->envc].* writes
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read/write on fixed-capacity array (unbounded index growth)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0022_cwe119.c (call sites) with the PUSH_ENV macro and envs allocation in the implementation (file not provided)
findings.invariant: h_env->envc must remain strictly within [0, h_env->nenv) before any envs[h_env->envc] access; every PUSH_ENV must be preceded by an 'h_env->envc_real < h_env->nenv' check (as HTML_DT/HTML_DD do at lines 273 and 292) or PUSH_ENV must clamp internally.
findings.failure_mode: An attacker page nests block containers deeply (chains of <ul>/<ol>/<blockquote>, also <dl>/<frameset>). Each open runs PUSH_ENV(cmd) with no nenv bound at these sites, advancing envc past the allocated nenv. The immediately following writes envs[h_env->envc].count/.type/.env and reads envs[h_env->envc].indent then touch memory beyond the envs[] allocation, giving an attacker-influenced out-of-bounds heap write and read from a hostile HTML document (memory corruption / potential control-flow impact). The DT/DD guard in the same function is direct proof PUSH_ENV does not self-clamp.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether the PUSH_ENV macro increments h_env->envc without checking against h_env->nenv, and how h_env->envs is sized (nenv). If PUSH_ENV lacks the bound, lines 136/180/328 are exploitable; the fix is to gate every PUSH_ENV on 'envc_real < nenv' or clamp inside the macro.

findings.title: Re-entrant HTMLlineproc1 feeding of attacker-derived strings (re-parse / uncontrolled recursion)
findings.anchor: HTMLlineproc1(tmp->ptr, h_env) on reconstructed title/isindex/meta/object strings
findings.bug_class: Uncontrolled recursion / re-parse of attacker content sharing layout state
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0022_cwe119.c (re-feed sites); HTMLlineproc1 and process_* in the implementation (file not provided)
findings.invariant: Text re-fed into HTMLlineproc1 must be inert with respect to re-openable block containers, or the HTML processing recursion depth must be bounded, so a hostile document cannot drive unbounded re-entrancy or compound the envs-stack growth.
findings.failure_mode: process_n_title / getMetaRefreshParam / ISINDEX build strings from attacker document content and re-inject them through HTMLlineproc1 (lines 321, 770, 808, and the BGSOUND/EMBED/APPLET/BODY links). HTMLlineproc1 re-lexes and re-dispatches into HTMLtagproc1 over the same obuf/envs/table_level state. If a re-fed fragment can re-expand into more containers/feedable text, processing recurses without an explicit cap, risking stack exhaustion and amplifying the unguarded env pushes of finding #1.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether HTMLlineproc1 can recurse into HTMLtagproc1 on the reconstructed strings and whether any global recursion/depth guard bounds it; confirm the reconstructed title/meta text cannot reintroduce <ul>/<dl>/<frameset> that push environments.

findings.title: Missing break causes unintended fall-through in HTML_N_DEL (NORMAL -> FONTIFY)
findings.anchor: case DISPLAY_INS_DEL_NORMAL with no break before DISPLAY_INS_DEL_FONTIFY
findings.invariant: Each display-mode case in the Ins/Del switches should terminate with break unless fall-through is intentional and documented; NORMAL mode must not execute the FONTIFY strike-close path.
findings.failure_mode: When displayInsDel == DISPLAY_INS_DEL_NORMAL, closing a </del> emits ':DEL]' and then falls through into the DISPLAY_INS_DEL_FONTIFY arm, running the in_strike decrement/close logic (and possibly emitting a spurious </s>) even though no strike was opened in NORMAL mode. This is a correctness bug producing wrong rendering/state; it is not memory-unsafe. Contrast HTML_N_S (lines 884-903) which breaks correctly.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether the fall-through is intentional; compare against the parallel HTML_N_S / HTML_N_INS handlers which each break after DISPLAY_INS_DEL_NORMAL. Add a break after line 853 if unintended.

audit_checklist: Read the PUSH_ENV/POP_ENV macros and the allocation of h_env->envs / value of h_env->nenv: confirm whether PUSH_ENV bounds h_env->envc against nenv, or whether the caller must (it must, per the DT/DD guards).

Add an 'envc_real < nenv' guard (or in-macro clamp) to the unguarded PUSH_ENV sites at lines 136 (UL/OL/BLQ), 180 (DL), and 328 (FRAMESET); fuzz with deeply nested <ul>/<blockquote>/<dl>/<frameset> under ASan.
Verify every envs[h_env->envc] and envs[h_env->envc - 1] access stays within [0, nenv): the envc-1 reads (lines 159, 191, 280) rely on the envc>0 guards remaining in place.

Trace HTMLlineproc1 re-entrancy from the derived-string re-feeds (lines 321, 770, 808, 965/975/985/995): confirm a recursion depth cap exists and that reconstructed text cannot reopen block environments.
Confirm parsedtag_get_value never returns true with a NULL/non-NUL-terminated pointer, since *p is dereferenced at lines 145, 231, and 505.
Decide whether the HTML_N_DEL NORMAL->FONTIFY fall-through (line 853) is intentional; add a break to match HTML_N_S/HTML_N_INS if not.
Confirm push_spaces treats a negative count (INDENT_INCR - num->length at line 255) as zero rather than converting to a large size_t.
